title: Chronology and Record
subtitle: Official submissions, minutes, and related documents
summary: A dated record of the main Unicode and related SignWriting documents relevant to current compatibility and standards discussions.
synopsis: This chronology collects the main Unicode proposals, support letters, collation responses, design notes, and related records from 2011 forward. It is meant as a stable reference trail for historians, standards readers, and anyone who needs the documentary path behind the current distinctions among character naming, stable symbol identity, and written-sign encoding.

slides.title: Why the Record Matters
slides.summary: The current position comes from a real document trail showing what advanced and what did not.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for chronology and record.
bullets: There were multiple proposals, responses, and minutes
The two-stage expectation matters
Character naming, symbol identity, and written-sign support did not advance equally
Collation and design concerns were documented
The documentary trail explains the present position
slides.speech: The current Unicode position did not emerge from one late complaint or one private disagreement. It developed across multiple proposals, support letters, minutes, technical responses, and later design discussions. The documentary trail matters because it shows what was expected, what changed, what concerns were raised, and what advanced and what did not. It also shows that character naming, stable symbol identity, and written-sign support did not advance equally.
slides.notes: Set the deck up as factual and documentary.

slides.title: 2011 to 2012: The Two-Stage Path
slides.summary: Early Unicode discussions proceeded under a two-stage expectation: first the symbol set, then a later solution for written signs.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the two-stage path.
bullets: Early proposals addressed symbol encoding
The expectation of a second stage for written signs was explicit
That expectation matters to later compatibility claims
It also helps explain later distinctions between characters, symbols, and written signs
The second stage never became a workable compatible standard
slides.speech: The early record matters because it reflects a two-stage expectation. First, encode the symbol set. Second, later address the encoding that takes symbols and turns them into signs. That distinction was important at the time, and it remains important now. It also helps explain a later distinction between naming characters, preserving stable symbols, and encoding complete written signs. The first stage moved forward. The second never became a workable compatible standard. That is one reason later claims of closure are hard to accept as technically adequate.
slides.notes: This is the chronology hinge. Keep the two-stage idea explicit.

slides.title: 2015: Block Approval Did Not End the Problem
slides.summary: The official block was approved, but stable symbol identity and written-sign questions remained active rather than resolved.
bullets: Unicode 8 added the SignWriting block
Character naming advanced
Collation concerns were immediately visible
The official approval did not settle symbol identity or written-sign compatibility
Block approval and problem resolution are not the same event
slides.speech: A common misunderstanding is that once the block was approved in Unicode 8, the technical problem was finished. The record shows otherwise. Character naming advanced, but collation concerns were active in 2015, and design questions remained active. Stable symbol identity and written-sign compatibility were not thereby settled. Approval of a block and resolution of compatibility are not the same thing. The chronology matters precisely because those ideas are often collapsed into one.
slides.notes: This slide separates approval from adequacy.

slides.title: 2016 to 2017: Renewed Design Work, No Durable Follow-Through
slides.summary: Later design presentations and options clarified the problem further, but they did not produce a completed compatible path.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for 2016 to 2017 design work.
bullets: Design notes and presentations continued
Auxiliary and alternative options were explored
The need for real technical support remained
The record ends in an unresolved position rather than a mature standard
slides.speech: The later record is not empty. There were further design notes, presentations, and attempts to clarify options. But those efforts did not produce a durable compatible follow-through. The chronology does not tell a story of a closed problem. It tells a story of a problem that remained technically live while institutional energy faded.
slides.notes: Keep this sober and documentary, not dramatic.

slides.title: What the Record Shows
slides.summary: The documentary trail supports a compatibility critique, not the story that Unicode fully solved the problem and only local reluctance remained.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what the record shows.
bullets: Character naming, stable symbol identity, and written-sign encoding did not advance equally
The symbol stage and the written-sign stage were distinct
Collation and model questions were real
Later technical work did not complete the path
The present position is historically grounded
slides.speech: The value of the record is not nostalgia. It is diagnostic clarity. The documentary trail supports the current compatibility critique. It shows a real two-stage expectation, real collation and model concerns, and later technical work that did not complete the path. It also shows that character naming, stable symbol identity, and written-sign encoding did not advance equally. That is why the present position should be read as historically grounded rather than as late-stage resistance.
slides.notes: Close with the interpretive value of the record.
